summary: On September 11, 2001, I was in the Washington Navy Yard working on plans for the upcoming World Bank and International Monetary Fund demonstrations when I first heard about the explosion at the World Trade Center. We quickly realized it was a coordinated terrorist attack after the second plane hit the towers. That day, I was involved in coordinating security for Washington, D.C., including the monuments and memorials, and responding to reports of further threats like the Pentagon attack and suspicious packages.

I first became aware of the terrorist attacks when I heard about an explosion at one of the towers of the World Trade Center. Watching the events unfold on television, including the shocking moment the second plane hit the second tower, made it clear this was a deliberate attack. This event triggered immediate action on our part to secure Washington, D.C., as we knew the capital was also a target.

At the time of the September 11 attacks, I was working out of temporary offices in Building 136 at the Washington Navy Yard. I was focused on planning for the upcoming World Bank and IMF demonstrations when I first heard about the explosion at the World Trade Center. From there, I quickly transitioned to coordinating security efforts across Washington, D.C., responding to the unfolding crisis.

In the weeks leading up to September 11, 2001, I was working on plans for the World Bank demonstrations scheduled for the end of that month. We anticipated potential violence based on experiences in other countries and were preparing accordingly. This planning was interrupted by the terrorist attacks, which shifted our focus entirely to emergency response and security in Washington, D.C.

Alongside the World Bank, I was involved in preparing for the International Monetary Fund demonstrations planned for late September 2001. We expected possible violent protests and had been working on security plans for months. The events of September 11 changed our priorities as we moved to respond to the terrorist attacks.

When the first reports of the World Trade Center explosion came in, we gathered around a television to watch the coverage, which I believe was on CNN or a local channel. Seeing the burning tower and then the second plane hit live was a shocking moment that confirmed this was a terrorist attack. CNN's coverage was crucial in informing us as the situation developed.

During the September 11 attacks, my role involved securing key locations throughout the District of Columbia, including the White House, monuments, and memorials. We coordinated evacuations and managed large crowds on the National Mall while dealing with communication challenges. The District was a primary focus because it was also a potential target, and we had to ensure public safety amid the chaos.

While driving from the Navy Yard to headquarters on September 11, I witnessed an explosion near the Pentagon and saw flames and smoke rising across the river. Officers near the site confirmed a plane had crashed into the building. We had to quickly respond by securing roadways leading to the Pentagon and assisting Arlington National Cemetery to prevent access to the area.

Deputy Chief Edward Winkel was acting as the Chief of Police on September 11, 2001, since the chief had retired. After the initial reports of the attacks, Major Pellinger contacted him, and Deputy Chief Winkel called us to headquarters to coordinate securing the monuments and memorials. He was a key figure in organizing the police response during those critical hours.

On September 11, securing the White House area was a top priority since the jurisdiction there falls under our responsibility. We worked closely with the Metropolitan Police and Secret Service to close several blocks to vehicles and pedestrians to ensure safety. I personally went to the White House area to oversee operations and ensure the perimeter was secure.

Captain Berberich and his officers had the Washington Monument area secured on September 11, 2001. They kept vehicles off the service roadway and maintained a perimeter with Jersey barricades to keep pedestrians at a safe distance. This was part of our broader effort to protect all the monuments and memorials during the attacks.

Captain Walters was responsible for coordinating the closure of the Lincoln Memorial area on September 11. We had officers stationed there to ensure the area was secure and evacuated as necessary. This was one of several memorials we focused on protecting during the heightened threat level that day.

We made sure the Jefferson Memorial was secured on September 11, 2001, although I don't recall exactly who was stationed there. It was part of our comprehensive effort to protect all major monuments and memorials in Washington, D.C. during the attacks. Ensuring these sites were safe was critical given the potential for further threats.

Lieutenant Markland was assigned to secure the Ellipse on September 11, 2001. This was part of our strategy to protect key areas around the White House and the National Mall. Securing the Ellipse helped us control access and maintain safety in the immediate vicinity of the presidential residence.

During the chaos of September 11, the National Mall was left open partly because it served as a shortcut for people trying to get to the Metro stations and across bridges. The Metro was a critical transit point for many evacuating or moving through the city. Managing pedestrian flow around Metro access points was an important part of our security efforts.

On September 11, we relied heavily on Nextel phones and radios for communication among officers, but the volume of transmissions overwhelmed the system and it went down temporarily. This made coordinating our response more challenging as officers arrived at various locations without clear assignments. The communication overload was a significant hurdle during the initial hours of the attack.

Rock Creek Parkway was a major focus for traffic management on September 11, 2001. Normally, its lanes change direction between morning and evening rush hours, and we implemented the evening outbound pattern early to help evacuate the city. We placed barricades and officers at key points to prevent drivers from going the wrong way and causing accidents during this unusual traffic flow.

Beach Drive was another critical roadway where we had to manage traffic carefully on September 11. We encountered a traffic signal issue at the intersection with Park Road that caused backups because it was not set for the emergency traffic pattern. This highlighted the need for better coordination across jurisdictions, which we addressed in later meetings with the Council of Governments.

The Theodore Roosevelt Bridge was one of the key access points where we placed barricades to control traffic flow during the emergency on September 11. We ensured officers were stationed to prevent vehicles from bypassing these barricades and causing dangerous situations. Managing these bridges was essential to maintaining order during the evacuation.

On September 11, there was a suspicious package reported on Memorial Bridge, which forced us to shut it down temporarily. This closure complicated traffic management because Memorial Bridge is a major artery out of the city. We had to balance security concerns with the need to keep traffic moving during the evacuation.

Following the events of September 11, the Council of Governments held meetings to improve coordination among jurisdictions for traffic and evacuation planning. We worked closely with them to develop plans that would allow us to manage large-scale evacuations more effectively, including traffic signal synchronization and roadway closures. Our police unit contributed by creating detailed plans for one-way traffic and truck restrictions around sensitive areas.

We have a memorandum of understanding with Arlington National Cemetery to provide law enforcement assistance there. On September 11, Arlington Cemetery and Arlington County requested our help to prevent people from moving from the cemetery into the Pentagon area. We deployed officers to secure the cemetery and nearby roadways to maintain safety and control access.

Route 110 in Virginia is one of the roadways we had to shut down on September 11 to prevent access to the Pentagon area. Controlling traffic on this route was part of our effort to secure the perimeter and manage the flow of vehicles during the emergency response. It was a critical piece of the broader security operation around the Pentagon.

While the transcript does not detail direct interaction, the Federal Bureau of Investigation was part of the broader law enforcement and investigative response following the September 11 attacks. Coordination with agencies like the FBI was essential in managing the security and investigation aspects of the terrorist attacks.

entity: United States Secretary of the Interior
summary: The transcript does not provide specific details about the United States Secretary of the Interior's role on September 11, but as the head of the department responsible for national parks and monuments, this office would have been involved in decisions about securing those sites during the attacks. Our police force worked to protect the monuments and memorials, which fall under the Interior's jurisdiction.

I was serving as assistant commander of the Special Forces Branch of the United States Park Police on September 11, 2001. Our unit was responsible for securing the monuments, memorials, and key areas in Washington, D.C., including the White House grounds. We coordinated evacuations, managed traffic, and worked with other agencies to protect the city during the attacks and the subsequent hours of uncertainty.

The Soviet Union was not mentioned in the context of the September 11 attacks or related events in the transcript. Therefore, I do not have any direct experience or involvement to share regarding the Soviet Union in relation to this oral history.
